content:
# Genesis Chapter 37 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 21v–22r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_037_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_037_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 35–36 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0039 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0040

---

*The Toledot of Jacob — Joseph at Seventeen (vv. 1–2)*

**37:1** And Jacob dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב יַעֲקֹב] in the land [בְּאֶרֶץ] of the sojournings of his father [מְגוּרֵי אָבִיו] — in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן].

**37:2** These are the generations [אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת] of Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] — Joseph [יוֹסֵף] — a son of seventeen years [בֶּן-שְׁבַע-עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה] — was shepherding [הָיָה רֹעֶה] the flock [אֶת-אֶחָיו] with his brothers [אֶת-אֶחָיו] — and he was a youth [וְהוּא נַעַר] with the sons of Bilhah [אֶת-בְּנֵי בִלְהָה] and with the sons of Zilpah [וְאֶת-בְּנֵי זִלְפָּה] — the wives of his father [נְשֵׁי אָבִיו] — and Joseph brought [וַיָּבֵא יוֹסֵף] their evil report [אֶת-דִּבָּתָם רָעָה] to their father [אֶל-אֲבִיהֶם].

---

*The Coat of Passim — The Brothers' Hatred (vv. 3–4)*

**37:3** And Israel loved [וְיִשְׂרָאֵל אָהַב] Joseph [אֶת-יוֹסֵף] more than all his sons [מִכָּל-בָּנָיו] — because [כִּי] he was a son of his old age [בֶּן-זְקֻנִים הוּא לוֹ] to him — and he made him [וְעָשָׂה לוֹ] a coat of passim [כְּתֹנֶת פַּסִּים].

**37:4** And his brothers saw [וַיִּרְאוּ אֶחָיו] that their father [כִּי-אֹתוֹ אָהַב אֲבִיהֶם] loved him [כִּי-אֹתוֹ אָהַב] more than all his brothers [מִכָּל-אֶחָיו] — and they hated him [וַיִּשְׂנְאוּ אֹתוֹ] — and they were not able [וְלֹא יָכְלוּ] to speak to him [דַּבְּרוֹ] in peace [לְשָׁלֹם].

---

*Joseph's First Dream — The Sheaves (vv. 5–8)*

**37:5** And Joseph dreamed [וַיַּחֲלֹם יוֹסֵף] a dream [חֲלוֹם] and he told [וַיַּגֵּד] his brothers [לְאֶחָיו] — and they added still more [וַיּוֹסִפוּ עוֹד] to hate him [שְׂנֹא אֹתוֹ].

**37:6** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to them [אֲלֵיהֶם] — Hear [שִׁמְעוּ-נָא] please [נָא] this dream [הַחֲלוֹם הַזֶּה] that I have dreamed [אֲשֶׁר חָלָמְתִּי].

**37:7** And behold [וְהִנֵּה] — we were binding [אֲנַחְנוּ מְאַלְּמִים] sheaves [אֲלֻמִּים] in the midst of the field [בְּתוֹךְ הַשָּׂדֶה] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — my sheaf [אֲלֻמָּתִי] arose [קָמָה] and also [וְגַם] stood upright [נִצָּבָה] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — your sheaves [אֲלֻמֹּתֵיכֶם] gathered around [תְּסֻבֶּינָה] and bowed down [וַתִּשְׁתַּחֲוֶיןָ] to my sheaf [לַאֲלֻמָּתִי].

**37:8** And his brothers said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוֹ אֶחָיו] to him — Will you indeed reign [הֲמָלֹךְ תִּמְלֹךְ] over us [עָלֵינוּ] — or will you indeed rule [אִם-מָשׁוֹל תִּמְשֹׁל] over us [בָּנוּ] — and they added still more [וַיּוֹסִפוּ עוֹד] to hate him [שְׂנֹא אֹתוֹ] on account of his dreams [עַל-חֲלֹמֹתָיו] and on account of his words [וְעַל-דְּבָרָיו].

---

*Joseph's Second Dream — Sun, Moon, and Stars (vv. 9–11)*

**37:9** And he dreamed [וַיַּחֲלֹם] yet another dream [עוֹד חֲלוֹם אַחֵר] and he recounted it [וַיְסַפֵּר אֹתוֹ] to his brothers [לְאֶחָיו] and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] — Behold [הִנֵּה] I have dreamed [חָלַמְתִּי] a dream again [עוֹד חֲלוֹם] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] the sun [הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ] and the moon [וְהַיָּרֵחַ] and eleven stars [וְאַחַד עָשָׂר כּוֹכָבִים] were bowing down [מִשְׁתַּחֲוִים] to me [לִי].

**37:10** And he recounted it [וַיְסַפֵּר] to his father [אֶל-אָבִיו] and to his brothers [וְאֶל-אֶחָיו] — and his father rebuked him [וַיִּגְעַר-בּוֹ אָבִיו] and said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ] — What is this dream [מַה הַחֲלוֹם הַזֶּה] that you have dreamed [אֲשֶׁר חָלָמְתָּ] — shall we indeed come [הֲבוֹא נָבוֹא] — I and your mother [אֲנִי וְאִמְּךָ] and your brothers [וְאַחֶיךָ] — to bow ourselves down [לְהִשְׁתַּחֲווֹת לְךָ] to you — to the ground [אַרְצָה]?

**37:11** And his brothers envied him [וַיְקַנְאוּ-בוֹ אֶחָיו] — but his father [וְאָבִיו] kept the matter [שָׁמַר אֶת-הַדָּבָר].

---

*Joseph Sent to His Brothers — Shechem to Dothan (vv. 12–17)*

**37:12** And his brothers went [וַיֵּלְכוּ אֶחָיו] to pasture [לִרְעוֹת] the flock of their father [אֶת-צֹאן אֲבִיהֶם] at Shechem [בִּשְׁכֶם].

**37:13** And Israel said [וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל] to Joseph [אֶל-יוֹסֵף] — Are not your brothers [הֲלוֹא אַחֶיךָ] pasturing [רֹעִים] at Shechem [בִּשְׁכֶם] — come [לְכָה] and I will send you [וְאֶשְׁלָחֲךָ] to them [אֲלֵיהֶם] — and he said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ] — Here I am [הִנֵּנִי].

**37:14** And he said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ] — Go [לֶךְ-נָא] please [נָא] and see [רְאֵה] the welfare [אֶת-שְׁלוֹם] of your brothers [אַחֶיךָ] and the welfare [וְאֶת-שְׁלוֹם] of the flock [הַצֹּאן] and bring back word to me [וַהֲשִׁבֵנִי דָבָר] — and he sent him [וַיִּשְׁלָחֵהוּ] from the valley of Hebron [מֵעֵמֶק חֶבְרוֹן] and he came [וַיָּבֹא] to Shechem [שְׁכֶמָה].

**37:15** And a man found him [וַיִּמְצָאֵהוּ אִישׁ] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] he was wandering [תֹּעֶה] in the field [בַּשָּׂדֶה] — and the man asked him [וַיִּשְׁאָלֵהוּ הָאִישׁ] — What are you seeking [מַה-תְּבַקֵּשׁ]?

**37:16** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] — I am seeking my brothers [אֶת-אַחַי אָנֹכִי מְבַקֵּשׁ] — tell me [הַגִּידָה-נָּא לִי] please [נָּא] — where [אֵיפֹה] are they pasturing [הֵם רֹעִים]?

**37:17** And the man said [וַיֹּאמֶר הָאִישׁ] — They have journeyed on [נָסְעוּ מִזֶּה] from here [מִזֶּה] — for I heard [כִּי שָׁמַעְתִּי] them saying [אֹמְרִים] — Let us go [נֵלְכָה] to Dothan [דֹּתָיְנָה] — and Joseph went [וַיֵּלֶךְ יוֹסֵף] after his brothers [אַחַר אֶחָיו] and he found them [וַיִּמְצָאֵם] at Dothan [בְּדֹתָן].

---

*The Brothers Plot Against Joseph — Reuben's Intervention (vv. 18–24)*

**37:18** And they saw him [וַיִּרְאוּ אֹתוֹ] from afar [מֵרָחֹק] — and before he drew near [וּבְטֶרֶם יִקְרַב אֲלֵיהֶם] to them — they plotted against him [וַיִּתְנַכְּלוּ אֹתוֹ] to kill him [לַהֲמִיתוֹ].

**37:19** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ] — each man to his brother [אִישׁ אֶל-אָחִיו] — Behold [הִנֵּה] — this master of dreams [בַּעַל הַחֲלֹמוֹת הַלָּזֶה] is coming [בָּא].

**37:20** And now [וְעַתָּה] — come [לְכוּ] and let us kill him [וְנַהַרְגֵהוּ] and let us throw him [וְנַשְׁלִכֵהוּ] into one of the pits [בְּאַחַד הַבֹּרוֹת] — and we shall say [וְאָמַרְנוּ] — A wild beast [חַיָּה רָעָה] has devoured him [אֲכָלָתְהוּ] — and we shall see [וְנִרְאֶה] what will become [מַה-יִּהְיוּ] of his dreams [חֲלֹמֹתָיו].

**37:21** And Reuben heard [וַיִּשְׁמַע רְאוּבֵן] and he delivered him [וַיַּצִּלֵהוּ] from their hand [מִיָּדָם] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] — Let us not strike him [לֹא נַכֶּנּוּ] fatally [נָפֶשׁ].

**37:22** And Reuben said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם רְאוּבֵן] to them — Shed no blood [אַל-תִּשְׁפְּכוּ-דָם] — throw him [הַשְׁלִיכוּ אֹתוֹ] into this pit [אֶל-הַבּוֹר הַזֶּה] that is in the wilderness [אֲשֶׁר בַּמִּדְבָּר] — but lay no hand [וְיָד אַל-תִּשְׁלְחוּ-בוֹ] upon him — so that he might rescue him [לְמַעַן הַצִּיל אֹתוֹ] from their hand [מִיָּדָם] to return him [לַהֲשִׁיבוֹ] to his father [אֶל-אָבִיו].

**37:23** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] when Joseph came [כַּאֲשֶׁר-בָּא יוֹסֵף] to his brothers [אֶל-אֶחָיו] — that they stripped [וַיַּפְשִׁיטוּ] Joseph [אֶת-יוֹסֵף] of his coat [אֶת-כֻּתָּנְתּוֹ] — the coat of passim [כְּתֹנֶת הַפַּסִּים] that was upon him [אֲשֶׁר עָלָיו].

**37:24** And they took him [וַיִּקָּחֻהוּ] and threw him [וַיַּשְׁלִכוּ אֹתוֹ] into the pit [הַבֹּרָה] — and the pit [וְהַבּוֹר] was empty [רֵק] — there was no water [אֵין בּוֹ מָיִם] in it.

---

*The Ishmaelite Caravan — Joseph Sold (vv. 25–28)*

**37:25** And they sat down [וַיֵּשְׁבוּ] to eat bread [לֶאֱכָל-לֶחֶם] — and they lifted up their eyes [וַיִּשְׂאוּ עֵינֵיהֶם] and they looked [וַיִּרְאוּ] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — a caravan of Ishmaelites [אֹרְחַת יִשְׁמְעֵאלִים] was coming [בָּאָה] from Gilead [מִגִּלְעָד] — and their camels [וּגְמַלֵּיהֶם] were bearing [נֹשְׂאִים] tragacanth and balm and laudanum [נְכֹאת וּצְרִי וָלֹט] — going [הוֹלְכִים] to bring down [לְהוֹרִיד] to Egypt [מִצְרָיְמָה].

**37:26** And Judah said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה] to his brothers [אֶל-אֶחָיו] — What profit [מַה-בֶּצַע] is it [כִּי נַהֲרֹג] if we kill [כִּי נַהֲרֹג] our brother [אֶת-אָחִינוּ] and cover [וְכִסִּינוּ] his blood [אֶת-דָּמוֹ]?

**37:27** Come [לְכוּ] and let us sell him [וְנִמְכְּרֶנּוּ] to the Ishmaelites [לַיִּשְׁמְעֵאלִים] — and let our hand [וְיָדֵנוּ] not be upon him [אַל-תְּהִי-בוֹ] — for he is our brother [כִּי-אָחִינוּ הוּא] — our own flesh [בְּשָׂרֵנוּ] — and his brothers listened [וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ אֶחָיו].

**37:28** And Midianite men [וַיַּעַבְרוּ אֲנָשִׁים מִדְיָנִים] passed by — merchants [סֹחֲרִים] — and they drew [וַיִּמְשְׁכוּ] and brought up [וַיַּעֲלוּ] Joseph [אֶת-יוֹסֵף] from the pit [מִן-הַבּוֹר] — and they sold [וַיִּמְכְּרוּ] Joseph [אֶת-יוֹסֵף] to the Ishmaelites [לַיִּשְׁמְעֵאלִים] for twenty pieces of silver [בְּעֶשְׂרִים כָּסֶף] — and they brought [וַיָּבִיאוּ] Joseph [אֶת-יוֹסֵף] to Egypt [מִצְרָיְמָה].

---

*Reuben Returns — The Pit Is Empty (vv. 29–30)*

**37:29** And Reuben returned [וַיָּשָׁב רְאוּבֵן] to the pit [אֶל-הַבּוֹר] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — Joseph was not [אֵין-יוֹסֵף] in the pit [בַּבּוֹר] — and he tore [וַיִּקְרַע] his garments [אֶת-בְּגָדָיו].

**37:30** And he returned [וַיָּשָׁב] to his brothers [אֶל-אֶחָיו] and said [וַיֹּאמַר] — The lad [הַיֶּלֶד] is gone [אֵינֶנּוּ] — and as for me [וַאֲנִי] — where [אָנָה] shall I go [אֲנִי בָא]?

---

*The Bloody Coat — Jacob's Grief (vv. 31–35)*

**37:31** And they took [וַיִּקְחוּ] the coat of Joseph [אֶת-כְּתֹנֶת יוֹסֵף] — and they slaughtered [וַיִּשְׁחֲטוּ] a male goat [שְׂעִיר עִזִּים] — and they dipped [וַיִּטְבְּלוּ] the coat [אֶת-הַכֻּתֹּנֶת] in the blood [בַּדָּם].

**37:32** And they sent [וַיְשַׁלְּחוּ] the coat of passim [אֶת-כְּתֹנֶת הַפַּסִּים] and brought it [וַיָּבִיאוּ] to their father [אֶל-אֲבִיהֶם] and they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ] — This we have found [זֹאת מָצָאנוּ] — recognize please [הַכֶּר-נָא] — is it your son's coat [הַכְּתֹנֶת בִּנְךָ הִוא] or not [אִם-לֹא]?

**37:33** And he recognized it [וַיַּכִּירָהּ] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] — It is my son's coat [כְּתֹנֶת בְּנִי] — a wild beast [חַיָּה רָעָה] has devoured him [אֲכָלָתְהוּ] — Joseph is surely torn [טָרֹף טֹרַף יוֹסֵף].

**37:34** And Jacob tore [וַיִּקְרַע יַעֲקֹב] his garments [שִׂמְלֹתָיו] and put [וַיָּשֶׂם] sackcloth [שַׂק] on his loins [בְּמָתְנָיו] and mourned [וַיִּתְאַבֵּל] for his son [עַל-בְּנוֹ] many days [יָמִים רַבִּים].

**37:35** And all his sons [וַיָּקֻמוּ כָל-בָּנָיו] and all his daughters [וְכָל-בְּנֹתָיו] rose up [וַיָּקֻמוּ] to comfort him [לְנַחֲמוֹ] — but he refused [וַיְמָאֵן] to be comforted [לְהִתְנַחֵם] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר] — For I will go down [כִּי-אֵרֵד] to my son [אֶל-בְּנִי] to Sheol [שְׁאֹלָה] mourning [אָבֵל] — and his father [וַיֵּבְךְּ אֹתוֹ אָבִיו] wept for him.

---

*Joseph Arrives in Egypt — Sold to Potiphar (v. 36)*

**37:36** And the Midianites [וְהַמְּדָנִים] sold him [מָכְרוּ אֹתוֹ] into Egypt [מִצְרָיְמָה] — to Potiphar [לְפוֹטִיפַר] — an officer of Pharaoh [סְרִיס פַּרְעֹה] — the captain of the guard [שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 21v–22r:**

Folio 21v (GEN_037_page_001.jpg) is the verso of the twenty-first leaf of the Leningrad Codex Genesis section, photographed in color by the West Semitic Research Project. The folio number in the upper margin is clearly legible. As noted in the preceding chapter's observations, the final verses of Genesis 36 occupy the opening columns of this folio — concluding with the territorial chiefs-list and the identification formula **הוּא עֵשָׂו אֲבִי אֱדוֹם** ("he is Esau, the father of Edom") — before the major *petuhah* (open section break) that marks the transition from the Esau-Edom narrative to the Jacob-Joseph story. This open-section break is one of the most visually significant transitions in the Genesis section of the Leningrad Codex: a full blank space in the running text signals the end of the genealogical chapters (Gen 36) and the beginning of the longest continuous narrative in Genesis (Gen 37–50). The three-column Tiberian-vocalized Sephardic square script resumes with the Joseph narrative at **וַיֵּשֶׁב יַעֲקֹב** ("and Jacob dwelt"). Full cantillation marks (*te'amim*) and vowel points (*niqud*) are present and legible throughout. The narrative text of Genesis 37 — with its dialogue, dramatic description, and direct speech — produces a visually denser, more varied line-rhythm on the folio compared to the genealogical lists of Genesis 36. Masorah parva notations are visible in the outer margin alongside specific verses; the masorah magna at the lower margin records chapter-level word-count data. The folio shows some vellum wear at the outer edges consistent with page-turning, but the text area is fully intact and legible.

Folio 22r (GEN_037_page_002.jpg) is the recto of the twenty-second leaf, carrying the latter half of Genesis 37 (approximately from verse 23 onward) through to the conclusion at verse 36, followed by the beginning of Genesis 38. The folio marker at the upper margin is visible. The three-column layout continues with the same consistent scribal hand. The dramatic narrative of Joseph's sale — the Ishmaelite caravan (v.25), the brothers' deliberation (vv.26–27), the Midianites' role (v.28), Reuben's discovery (vv.29–30), the bloody coat episode (vv.31–35), and the final verse placing Joseph in Potiphar's house (v.36) — is all fully legible. A *setumah* (closed section break) space is visible before verse 36, or within the transitional movement from the Jacob-mourning scene to the Potiphar notice, reflecting the scribe's structural parsing of the narrative. The right column of this folio carries the beginning of Genesis 38 (the Judah-Tamar episode), marked by a section break. No lacunae or damage affects the text of Genesis 37 on this folio.

---

**37:1–2 — *toledot Ya'aqov* (תֹּלְדוֹת יַעֲקֹב) — "These Are the Generations of Jacob": The Paradox of the Toledot Heading**

Verse 2 opens with the standard *toledot* formula: **אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת יַעֲקֹב** — "these are the generations of Jacob." This is the eleventh and final use of the *toledot* formula in Genesis (Genesis 2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 10:1; 11:10; 11:27; 25:12; 25:19; 36:1; 36:9; 37:2), and it contains one of the most arresting structural paradoxes in the entire book. The formula introduces a section, and the named subject is expected to be the protagonist of what follows — as "the generations of Terah" introduces the Abraham story (11:27), "the generations of Isaac" introduces the Jacob story (25:19), and so forth. Here, "the generations of Jacob" immediately pivots to Joseph: **יוֹסֵף בֶּן-שְׁבַע-עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה** ("Joseph, a son of seventeen years"). The entire subsequent narrative (Genesis 37–50) is about Joseph, not Jacob in any primary sense.

The paradox is theologically deliberate. Jacob *is* the subject of the *toledot* — but the toledot of a patriarch means "what he produced": his descendants, his legacy. What Jacob produced, supremely, was Joseph. And the Joseph narrative is the fullest account of what the Abrahamic promise looks like lived out in the world — in a foreign land, through suffering, through ambiguity. The *toledot* formula frames the Joseph narrative as the ultimate expression of Jacob/Israel's lineage. The nation is not yet born; the family is on the verge of descending to Egypt. The Jacob-toledot introduces that entire trajectory.

Verse 1 sets the stage with geographical precision: **בְּאֶרֶץ מְגוּרֵי אָבִיו בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן** — "in the land of the sojournings of his father, in the land of Canaan." The word **מְגוּרִים** (*megurim*, "sojournings") is the noun from the root גּוּר (*gur*, "to sojourn, to dwell as a stranger"), the same root that describes the patriarchal existence throughout Genesis. Jacob is a sojourner in Canaan — as his father Isaac was, as Abraham was. The land has been promised but not possessed. The contrast with the preceding chapter — Esau's Edom, with its kings and chiefs and territorial settlements — is deliberate: Esau has his land, his *achuzzah*; Jacob's family remains in the posture of sojourners.

---

**37:2 — *ve-hu na'ar* (וְהוּא נַעַר) — "He Was a Youth": Joseph's Age and Status**

The word **נַעַר** (*na'ar*) — rendered here as "youth" — has a semantic range that extends from "young man" to "servant/attendant" in the Hebrew Bible. The ambiguity is productive in the Joseph story. Joseph at seventeen is a *na'ar* — not a full adult, not yet established in his own household, still subject to his father's authority. The term's secondary connotation of "servant/attendant" will echo through the story: Joseph the *na'ar* with his brothers will become the *na'ar* serving in Potiphar's house (Gen 39:11), and later the Pharaoh's second-in-command. The descent and ascent of the *na'ar* is the shape of the Joseph narrative.

The phrase **אֶת-בְּנֵי בִלְהָה וְאֶת-בְּנֵי זִלְפָּה נְשֵׁי אָבִיו** ("with the sons of Bilhah and with the sons of Zilpah, the wives of his father") specifies that Joseph worked alongside the sons of the concubine-wives, not alongside the full brothers (sons of Leah). The sons of Bilhah are Dan and Naphtali; the sons of Zilpah are Gad and Asher. Joseph's working relationship is with these four — the secondary sons of the household. This detail foreshadows the narrative's social dynamics: the full sons of Leah (Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun) are conspicuously absent from this pastoral assignment. It is among the half-brothers of lower status that Joseph functions as *na'ar*.

---

**37:2 — *dibat ahem ra'ah* (דִּבָּתָם רָעָה) — "Their Evil Report": What Did Joseph Report?**

**דִּבָּה** (*dibbah*) is a significant term: it means "report, whispering, tale" — most often with a negative connotation of slander or ill-report (Numbers 13:32, where the spies bring back an evil report of the land; Proverbs 10:18; 25:10). The construction **דִּבָּתָם רָעָה** means "their evil report" or "a bad report about them" — the suffix *-am* ("their") is objective, not possessive: this is a report *about* them, not a report *they* made. Joseph, in other words, was bringing a negative account of his brothers to Jacob. The text does not specify what the content was. This has generated extensive interpretive tradition: Rashi, following the Midrash (Bereishit Rabbah 84:7), identifies the report's content as three specific accusations — that the sons of Leah were eating limb torn from living animals, that they were demeaning the sons of the handmaidens, and that they were involved in sexual impropriety. The text itself leaves the content unspecified, which is narratively important: the reader does not know whether Joseph's report was accurate. Was he a loyal son conveying genuine problems, or a self-serving informer? The narrative's deliberate reticence on this point invites both sympathy and unease toward the young Joseph.

---

**37:3 — *ketonet passim* (כְּתֹנֶת פַּסִּים) — The Garment: Three Centuries of Translation Controversy**

No single phrase in Genesis 37 has generated more sustained scholarly, linguistic, and cultural debate than **כְּתֹנֶת פַּסִּים** (*ketonet passim*) — the garment Jacob makes for Joseph. The word **כְּתֹנֶת** (*kutonet*) is clear: it is a tunic or long robe, the fundamental garment of the ancient Near Eastern wardrobe. The problem is **פַּסִּים** (*passim*), which is a hapax legomenon in this specific adjectival function — it appears nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible as a garment descriptor.

The major interpretive options, each with ancient manuscript support:

**1. "Coat of many colors" (KJV, following the LXX and Peshitta):**
The LXX Codex Vaticanus at 37:3 reads **χιτῶνα ποικίλον** (*chitōna poikilon*) — "an ornamented / multi-colored tunic." The word *poikilos* in Greek means "variegated, multi-colored, embroidered." This is the source of the KJV's famous "coat of many colors" and of Tissot's celebrated Victorian painting. The LXX translation appears to interpret *passim* as relating to variegated decoration or multicolored fabric. The Peshitta Syriac similarly renders it as a colored/ornamented garment.

**2. "Long-sleeved robe" (RSV, NRSV, NIV margin):**
This interpretation connects **פַּסִּים** (*passim*) to the noun **פַּס** (*pas*) meaning "palm (of the hand)" or "sole (of the foot)" — i.e., the extremities. A *ketonet passim* on this reading is a garment extending to the palms and soles — a full-length, long-sleeved tunic reaching to the wrists and ankles. Such a garment would be the attire of a non-laboring person, an aristocrat or official, in contrast to the shorter working garments of laborers. This interpretation is supported by the only other occurrence of the same phrase in the Hebrew Bible: **2 Samuel 13:18–19**, where Tamar daughter of David wears a **כְּתֹנֶת הַפַּסִּים** — described as a garment worn by "the virgin daughters of the king." This is strong contextual evidence: if the royal daughters of David wore this garment, it is a garment of high status, not merely of many colors. That it is worn by Tamar and given to Joseph — both figures who will suffer sexual or social violation in their narratives — creates an intertextual connection of garment and vulnerability.

**3. "Ornamented tunic / richly embroidered coat" (various modern translations):**
Some scholars connect *passim* to Akkadian *pisannu* or to a root meaning "to spread out, to extend" — yielding a sense of an elaborately decorated, embroidered garment. The Vulgate renders it *tunica polymita* — a tunic woven of many threads, an artisan-quality fabric.

**4. Aramaic cognate — "pieces/strips":**
An Aramaic cognate for *pas* meaning "piece" or "strip" suggests a garment made of strips of fabric, perhaps suggesting patchwork or a coat assembled from many sewn panels.

**EMET's position:** The *ketonet passim* is best understood as a garment of elite social status — a long, full-coverage tunic inappropriate for physical labor, marking its wearer as someone exempt from field work. Whether the elite quality derived from its multicolored decoration, its full-length cut, or its embroidered fabric is secondary to its social function: the garment visually marks Joseph as Jacob's favored heir. The brothers see it and understand immediately: Joseph has been designated above them. EMET retains the Hebrew term in all occurrences (vv. 3, 23, 32) and renders it "coat of passim" to preserve the untranslatable specificity. The 2 Samuel 13 parallel is the strongest contextual anchor for understanding the garment's significance.

---

**37:3 — *ben-zekunim hu lo* (בֶּן-זְקֻנִים הוּא לוֹ) — "Son of His Old Age"**

The phrase **בֶּן-זְקֻנִים** (*ben-zekunim*, "son of old age") is significant. Literally it means "a son of old-ages to him" — a child born to Jacob in his old age, uniquely associated with the end of his life. Benjamin was in fact the youngest son (born after Genesis 37, and nearly at the cost of Rachel's life, Gen 35:16–18), but the narrative consistently frames Joseph as the beloved son of Jacob's old age. The reason is Rachel: Joseph is the firstborn of Rachel, Jacob's true-beloved wife (Gen 29:18). After fourteen years of labor and the years of childbearing competition, Joseph is the son for whom Jacob had worked. His birth is a kind of culmination of the Rachel-love story. The text's identification of Joseph as *ben-zekunim* has been interpreted two ways in rabbinic tradition: (1) literally, as a son born in old age, and (2) as a son to whom Jacob transmitted his accumulated wisdom — *zekunim* understood as a play on *zaken* ("elder, wise one"), so that Joseph was a receptacle of the elder's learning.

---

**37:4 — *ve-lo yakhlu dabro le-shalom* (וְלֹא יָכְלוּ דַּבְּרוֹ לְשָׁלֹם) — "They Were Not Able to Speak to Him in Peace"**

This is one of the most psychologically precise sentences in Genesis. The brothers did not merely dislike Joseph — they were **not able** (לֹא יָכְלוּ, "they were not able, they could not") to speak to him **in peace** (לְשָׁלֹם). The word **שָׁלוֹם** (*shalom*) in direct speech context means the standard greeting of well-being — "how are you, are you well." The brothers cannot even manage a civil greeting. Their hatred has so consumed them that ordinary social speech — *shalom*, the everyday word of peace and welfare — is impossible. The narrator's observation is devastating in its precision: this is not merely tension; this is complete social rupture. There is no shalom between Joseph and his brothers.

The note that this incapacity follows specifically from their seeing that their father **loved him more than all his brothers** — the Hebrew construction **כִּי-אֹתוֹ אָהַב אֲבִיהֶם מִכָּל-אֶחָיו** ("that him their father loved more than all his brothers") places the object "him" in emphatic first position in the clause — grammatically underlining the exclusivity of Jacob's love. The syntax enacts the favoritism the sentence describes.

---

**37:5–8 — The Sheaf Dream: Literary Structure and Foreshadowing**

The Joseph narrative contains a carefully constructed system of paired dreams. Joseph has two dreams (vv.5–8, 9–11) that his brothers and father interpret as claims to dominion. Later, the cupbearer and baker in prison each dream (Gen 40), and Joseph interprets their paired dreams. Finally, Pharaoh has two dreams (Gen 41) that Joseph interprets as one revelation. The doubling-pattern is explicit in Genesis 41:32: "the dream was repeated to Pharaoh twice because the thing is fixed by God and God will soon act on it." The first pair of Joseph's own dreams establishes this structure from the outset.

The first dream is specifically agricultural: **אֲלֻמִּים** (*alumim*, "sheaves") — from the root אָלַם (*alam*, "to bind together"). The verbal form **מְאַלְּמִים** (*me'allemim*, "we were binding, binding-up") is a Piel participle, intensive form, emphasizing the active work of binding sheaves in the harvest. The sheaves bow down: **וַתִּשְׁתַּחֲוֶיןָ לַאֲלֻמָּתִי** — the Hishtaphel form of שָׁחָה (*shachah*), the verb for prostration in worship or obeisance. The dream casts the brothers as agricultural laborers whose harvest-work ultimately serves and honors Joseph's sheaf. The brothers understand the dream's meaning immediately: "will you indeed reign over us?"

The double infinitive absolute construction **הֲמָלֹךְ תִּמְלֹךְ** ("will you indeed reign") followed by **אִם-מָשׁוֹל תִּמְשֹׁל** ("or will you indeed rule") uses the rhetorical emphasis of the infinitive absolute to express incredulous disbelief — the brothers' question is not a real question but a horrified exclamation. The two verbs — **מָלַךְ** (*malakh*, "to reign, be king") and **מָשַׁל** (*mashal*, "to rule, govern") — are near-synonyms, but *malakh* refers specifically to royal kingship while *mashal* refers more broadly to dominion and governance. The brothers invoke both: will you be king? Will you rule us? The dream's full social implication is understood and resented.

---

**37:9–11 — The Sun, Moon, and Stars Dream: A Mother Problem**

The second dream is cosmic: **הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ וְהַיָּרֵחַ וְאַחַד עָשָׂר כּוֹכָבִים** ("the sun and the moon and eleven stars") — eleven brothers bowing, plus the sun (father) and moon (mother). Jacob's immediate interpretation is clear: "shall I and *your mother* and your brothers come to bow down to you?" — the sun = Jacob, the moon = Rachel (or a maternal figure), the eleven stars = eleven brothers.

But Rachel is dead. She died at the birth of Benjamin (Genesis 35:19). At the time of this dream, Joseph's biological mother is dead and buried on the road to Bethlehem. This creates a famous interpretive difficulty: how does Jacob's interpretation assign the moon to Joseph's mother if Joseph's mother is deceased? Rabbinic interpretation (Rashi, following Bereishit Rabbah 84:11) proposes that the "mother" symbolized is Bilhah — Rachel's handmaid who raised Joseph after Rachel's death, effectively functioning as his surrogate mother. Others suggest that Jacob's interpretation is deliberate misdirection to deflect the dream's weight, or that the moon simply represents "a mother-figure" without specifying which one. The Midrash notes that the verse adds: "and his father kept the matter" — implying Jacob understood the dream's real force even while rebuking Joseph publicly.

What is narratively crucial is that this dream is fulfilled in an altered form: the brothers do bow down to Joseph in Egypt (Gen 42:6), but Jacob himself does not bow before the narrative's end — at least not in explicitly fulfilling the dream's terms. The sun-moon-stars image exceeds what the narrative literally depicts, suggesting that the dream contains a surplus of meaning pointing beyond the immediate story.

---

**37:11 — *shamar et ha-davar* (שָׁמַר אֶת-הַדָּבָר) — "His Father Kept the Matter"**

The phrase **וְאָבִיו שָׁמַר אֶת-הַדָּבָר** ("and his father kept the matter") is syntactically placed in contrast to the brothers' envy: "his brothers envied him — *but* his father kept the matter." The verb **שָׁמַר** (*shamar*) means "to keep, guard, watch over, preserve." The object **הַדָּבָר** (*ha-davar*) means "the word, the thing, the matter." Together: Jacob "kept the word/matter" — he held the dream in his mind, preserved it, did not dismiss it.

The famous parallel is Luke 2:19 and 2:51, where Mary "kept all these things, pondering them in her heart" (*synetērei panta ta rhēmata en tē kardia autēs*) — the same narrative gesture of a parent holding in memory a striking thing said about a child. The Lukan phrase appears to echo this Genesis passage deliberately, placing Mary in the tradition of those who recognize that extraordinary words about a child may contain a prophetic weight too large to process immediately. Jacob's *shamar* is the posture of a man who does not know quite what the dream means but senses that it matters. The brothers envied; the father waited.

---

**37:12–17 — Joseph's Journey: The Valley of Hebron to Dothan**

The geographical progression of this section — Hebron, Shechem, Dothan — is narratively and geographically plausible. Hebron (modern Khalil) is in the southern hill country; Shechem (modern Nablus) is in the central hill country, approximately 60 miles north of Hebron. Dothan (modern Tell Dothan) is a further 15 miles north of Shechem, in the Dothan valley — a major route between the hill country and the major roads leading to Egypt. The geographical detail functions both realistically (the journey is plausible) and symbolically: Joseph travels north from his father's home at Hebron, ever further into the territory of his brothers' operations, and ultimately into the hands of a southward-moving caravan that will carry him to Egypt.

The anonymous **אִישׁ** (*ish*, "man") of verse 15 who redirects Joseph from Shechem to Dothan has been the subject of speculation in rabbinic and later tradition. The man finds Joseph "wandering" (*to'eh*, from the root תָּעָה, *ta'ah*, "to wander, go astray, err") in the field — a verb with overtones of lostness both physical and perhaps moral. The man directs Joseph to Dothan with information that proves accurate: "they have journeyed on, for I heard them saying, let us go to Dothan." In rabbinic midrash, the anonymous man is sometimes identified as the angel Gabriel — a divine agent ensuring that Joseph reaches his brothers, so that the divine plan of the Egyptian sojourn can be set in motion.

The word **הִנֵּנִי** (*hineni*, "here I am / behold me") — Joseph's response to his father's summons in verse 13 — is one of the charged words of the Hebrew Bible. Abraham uses it in Genesis 22:1 when called by God to the *aqedah*. Isaac uses it in Genesis 22:7 when he asks his father about the offering. Jacob uses it in Genesis 31:11 when the angel speaks to him. **הִנֵּנִי** is the word of total availability, of presented self, of readiness for whatever is demanded. Joseph's use of it here — in response to his father's summons to go to his brothers — is laced with dramatic irony: Joseph is fully available, fully compliant, fully ready. He does not know what he is walking toward.

---

**37:18–20 — The Conspiracy: *Baal ha-chalomot* (בַּעַל הַחֲלֹמוֹת)**

**בַּעַל הַחֲלֹמוֹת הַלָּזֶה** ("this master of dreams") — the brothers' scornful designation for Joseph — is one of the narratively richest phrases in the chapter. **בַּעַל** (*ba'al*) is the word for "master, owner, possessor, husband" — and also the Canaanite deity Baal. A *ba'al* of something owns it, is defined by it. The brothers mean it as a mockery: "this dreamer, this fantasy-master." But the irony is that the title is accurate. Joseph is indeed the *ba'al ha-chalomot* — the one to whom dreams belong, who possesses the gift of dreaming and of interpreting. The brothers' contempt is the vehicle for an accurate description. Their taunt is his title.

Verse 20's **וְנִרְאֶה מַה-יִּהְיוּ חֲלֹמֹתָיו** ("and we shall see what will become of his dreams") is one of the finest instances of dramatic irony in biblical narrative. The brothers intend it as a terminating statement: once Joseph is dead, his dreams will be nullified — "let us see what becomes of his ridiculous dreams then." But the irony is structural: the reader, knowing the Joseph story, reads this as an unwitting prophecy. We *will* see what becomes of his dreams. The answer is: they come entirely true. The brothers' derisive challenge to Joseph's dreams becomes the implicit question of the entire narrative: what *will* become of his dreams? Answer: Genesis 39–50.

---

**37:21–22 — Reuben's Intervention: *lo nakkenu nefesh* (לֹא נַכֶּנּוּ נָפֶשׁ)**

Reuben's intervention is the first act of moral resistance in the narrative, and it is linguistically precise. **לֹא נַכֶּנּוּ נָפֶשׁ** — the phrase is literally "let us not strike him [as to] life/soul" — *nefesh* functions here as an accusative of specification, indicating the lethal degree of striking. Reuben is saying: whatever we do, do not kill him. The narrator then discloses Reuben's private motive: **לְמַעַן הַצִּיל אֹתוֹ מִיָּדָם לַהֲשִׁיבוֹ אֶל-אָבִיו** ("so that he might rescue him from their hand, to return him to his father"). Reuben's plan is to use the pit as a holding place — he intends to come back later and retrieve Joseph. The plan is never executed: by the time Reuben returns, Joseph is gone (v.29).

The character of Reuben in this chapter is the character of the eldest son who lacks authority. He wants to save Joseph, but he cannot simply command his brothers not to harm Joseph. He maneuvers. He proposes an indirect solution (the pit). He departs — the text does not explain why — and when he returns, the moment has passed. Reuben's moral instinct is right; his execution is ineffective. His anguish in verse 30 ("the lad is gone — and as for me, where shall I go?") is the lament of a man who meant to do right and failed anyway.

---

**37:25–28 — The Ishmaelite/Midianite Problem: One of the Most Discussed Cruxes in Genesis**

Verses 25–28 present one of the most persistent textual difficulties in the Joseph narrative. The problem is simply stated:

- **Verse 25:** A caravan of **Ishmaelites** (*yishme'elim*) is sighted, coming from Gilead.
- **Verse 26–27:** Judah proposes selling Joseph to **the Ishmaelites** (*la-yishme'elim*).
- **Verse 28:** **Midianite men** (*anashim midyanim*) pass by, pull Joseph from the pit, and sell him to **the Ishmaelites** (*la-yishme'elim*) for twenty pieces of silver. Joseph is then brought to Egypt.
- **Verse 36:** **The Midianites** (*ha-medanim*) sold Joseph in Egypt to Potiphar.

The compression of the verse creates an unstable set of agents. Three distinct readings have been proposed:

**1. The Ishmaelites and Midianites are the same people / loosely interchangeable terms:**
This is the oldest harmonizing interpretation, adopted by many classical commentators. In the ancient Near East, caravan traders of various tribal affiliations could be called by either ethnic label: both Ishmaelites and Midianites were trading peoples of the northern Arabian/Transjordanian region, and "Ishmaelite" may have been used loosely to describe traders of this general type. Judges 8:24 appears to use "Ishmaelite" for people who are explicitly the Midianites defeated by Gideon, which some take as evidence that the terms could overlap. On this reading there is no contradiction.

**2. Source-critical division (J and E):**
The classic documentary approach (Wellhausen and subsequent scholarship) divides the passage between two distinct source documents. In the J (Yahwist) source, Judah proposes selling to the Ishmaelites (vv.26–27), the brothers sell Joseph to the Ishmaelite caravan (v.28a, partially), and v.36 reflects J's account of the sale. In the E (Elohist) source, the Midianites (not the brothers) independently pull Joseph from the pit and sell him to the Ishmaelites without the brothers' knowledge or participation — accounting for why Reuben returns to find Joseph gone and is genuinely shocked (v.29), and why the brothers are later uncertain about exactly what happened (Gen 42:21–22, where they recall "his distress when he pleaded with us" but say nothing about having sold him). On this reading, the E-source narrative has the Midianites taking Joseph while the brothers were elsewhere, which is why Reuben — who had stepped away — finds the pit empty.

**3. Harmonizing narrative reading:**
The Midianites *pulled Joseph out of the pit* on their own initiative, independently of the brothers' plan; the brothers, watching from a distance, then sold him to the Ishmaelite caravan as the Midianites passed. Or alternatively, "Midianite merchants" is a further specification of who within the Ishmaelite caravan actually conducted the transaction — the Midianite traders were part of the larger Ishmaelite trading group.

No reading is entirely without difficulty. EMET translates the text as it stands in the Leningrad Codex without harmonizing: verse 28 uses *Midianites* and *Ishmaelites* in the same verse; verse 36 uses *Midianites* (with the variant **מְדָנִים** *medanim* rather than **מִדְיָנִים** *midyanim* — a textual variant within the MT itself, noted in the LC). The textual ambiguity is part of the narrative's historical record and may itself be ancient, preserved as a seam between oral and written traditions.

**The twenty pieces of silver (עֶשְׂרִים כָּסֶף):** The price for Joseph is twenty shekels of silver — the standard price for a young male slave in the ancient Near East, consistent with the Middle Bronze Age slave-price evidence from Nuzi and other sources. (Later OT texts set slave prices higher: Exodus 21:32 values a slave's life at 30 shekels; the later price for a young adult male in Leviticus 27:5 is 20 shekels, for an adult male 50 shekels.) The specificity of the price gives the transaction a historical-commercial texture: this is a real economic transaction at a historically plausible rate.

---

**37:25 — The Caravan's Cargo: *Nekho't u-tseri va-lot* (נְכֹאת וּצְרִי וָלֹט)**

The three commodities the Ishmaelite caravan carries from Gilead to Egypt — **נְכֹאת** (*nekho't*), **צְרִי** (*tseri*), and **לֹט** (*lot*) — are traded spices and medicinal resins of the ancient Near Eastern luxury trade:

- **נְכֹאת** (*nekho't*): Traditionally translated "spicery" or "tragacanth" — a gum resin from the *Astragalus* shrub, used in perfumery and medicine. The Talmud discusses it; the exact species identification remains debated. Some translators render "gum/aromatic."
- **צְרִי** (*tseri*): "Balm of Gilead" — a healing resin, the famous *balsam* associated with Gilead (cf. Jeremiah 8:22: "Is there no balm in Gilead?"). Used medicinally and as a perfume base.
- **לֹט** (*lot*): "Laudanum" — the gum resin of the *Cistus* shrub, used in ancient perfumery and as a fixative in incense. Not to be confused with the opium-derived laudanum of later Western medicine, though the name is related through Arabic *ladan*.

The detail of the spice-bearing caravan is not ornamental. It grounds the narrative geographically and commercially in the specific trade routes of the ancient Near East — the Gilead-to-Egypt route was one of the great trading arteries of the Bronze Age, carrying luxury goods from Mesopotamia and the Levant to the Egyptian market. That Joseph should be traded *on this road, by these traders, alongside these goods* is both historically precise and thematically resonant: Joseph — himself a kind of rare, costly, prized thing — is carried to Egypt in the company of the most valued commodities of his world.

---

**37:29–30 — Reuben's Return: *ha-yeled einenu* (הַיֶּלֶד אֵינֶנּוּ)**

Reuben's return to the pit and his cry — **הַיֶּלֶד אֵינֶנּוּ** ("the lad is gone / the lad is not") — shifts the vocabulary from **נַעַר** (*na'ar*, "youth/servant") used in verse 2 to **יֶלֶד** (*yeled*, "child, boy"). Reuben's word is the intimate, diminutive term — the child, the boy. This registers the emotional register of Reuben's panic: in his distress he does not say "Joseph" or "my brother" but simply "the child." The word **אֵינֶנּוּ** (*einenu*, "he is not, he is gone") — from אֵין (*ein*, "there is not") + the third-person masculine suffix — is a stark idiom of absence. The same word will echo through the chapter: Jacob uses it in his grief implicitly in the absence of Joseph, and it will appear again in Egypt when the brothers say "one is gone" (Gen 42:13: **וְהָאֶחָד אֵינֶנּוּ**).

Reuben's **וַאֲנִי אָנָה אֲנִי-בָא** ("and as for me, where shall I go?") is a cry of personal devastation. The rhetorical question is not about literal destination — it is about consequences. Reuben, as the firstborn, bears special responsibility for Joseph's welfare. His failure is personal and familial and existential. The doubled pronoun **אֲנִי אָנָה אֲנִי** ("as for me, where I") is intensively self-focused — the sentence circles back to *I* and its implications twice. Reuben is the eldest son who was supposed to protect the family's youngest beloved child, and he has failed.

---

**37:31–33 — The Deception: *Haker na* (הַכֶּר-נָא)**

The phrase **הַכֶּר-נָא** (*haker-na*, "recognize please / identify this") in verse 32 is a devastating narrative echo. In Genesis 38:25 — in the very next chapter — Tamar uses the *identical phrase* to Judah when she presents his seal and cord: "recognize please — whose are these?" Here in verse 32, the brothers use the phrase to Jacob: "recognize please — is this your son's coat?" In chapter 38, Judah must recognize his own pledged items and acknowledge a truth he had concealed. The same word of recognition, the same narrative gesture of presenting an object for identification, frames Judah in both chapters: in Genesis 37 he is the one using the deceptive recognition-demand on his father; in Genesis 38 he is the victim of the same device. The narrative's use of *haker na* across chapters 37 and 38 constitutes a literary hinge — Judah's deception of his father is mirrored and answered by Tamar's use of the same mechanism on Judah.

Jacob's response in verse 33 — **חַיָּה רָעָה אֲכָלָתְהוּ טָרֹף טֹרַף יוֹסֵף** — employs the infinitive absolute again: **טָרֹף טֹרַף** ("surely torn, torn to pieces") — the intensive form expressing absolute certainty. Jacob arrives at the exact conclusion the brothers had planted: "a wild beast has devoured him; Joseph is surely torn." The deception succeeds completely. Jacob does not suspect his sons; he constructs a narrative of animal predation from the blood-soaked coat — the brothers' calculated manipulation of his paternal fears.

---

**37:34–35 — Jacob's Grief: The Consolation That Cannot Be Accepted**

**וַיִּקְרַע יַעֲקֹב שִׂמְלֹתָיו וַיָּשֶׂם שַׂק בְּמָתְנָיו** — Jacob tears his garments and puts on sackcloth. The tearing of garments (**קָרַע**, *qara'*) is the standard ancient Israelite gesture of grief and mourning (cf. Joshua 7:6; 2 Samuel 1:11; Job 1:20). Sackcloth (**שַׂק**, *saq*) — coarse cloth, probably woven from goat or camel hair — was the mourning garment, the visible sign of grief worn against the body. Jacob enters formal, visible mourning.

The phrase **וַיְמָאֵן לְהִתְנַחֵם** ("and he refused to be comforted") is psychologically acute. All the children — sons and daughters — rise to comfort him, but Jacob refuses comfort. His reasoning, given in verse 35, is: **כִּי-אֵרֵד אֶל-בְּנִי אָבֵל שְׁאֹלָה** — "for I will go down to my son to Sheol mourning." Jacob refuses comfort because he has structured his grief teleologically: he will mourn until he joins Joseph in death. There is no comfort because there is no future in which Joseph is restored. Jacob's refusal of comfort is not mere despair; it is a declaration of permanent mourning — a decision to carry the grief of Joseph's "death" to his own grave.

The devastating irony is that Jacob is wrong on every point. Joseph is not dead. Sheol is not Joseph's destination. Jacob will see his son again. But Jacob does not know this, and the gap between what Jacob believes and what the reader of the larger narrative knows creates one of the most poignant dramatic ironies in Genesis. Jacob's grief, though based on a lie, is real grief. His inconsolability is genuine — and it will make his joy in Genesis 45–46 all the more overwhelming.

---

**37:35 — *She'olah* (שְׁאֹלָה) — Sheol: The First Explicit Genesis Reference**

The word **שְׁאוֹל** (*She'ol*) — rendered here "Sheol" — appears here for the first time in Genesis, and this is the only occurrence of the word in the book. Genesis 42:38 will use it again (Jacob's lament about Benjamin: "you would bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to Sheol"), and 44:29, 31 follow. The word means the realm of the dead, the underworld, the place all mortals descend to at death. It is not a place of punishment or reward in the early Hebrew conception — it is simply *where the dead are*. The etymology of *She'ol* is uncertain: proposals include connection to the root שָׁאַל (*sha'al*, "to ask, inquire") — the underworld as the place of inquiry or the place that "asks" for the living; or to a root related to "hollowness, depth."

Theologically, Sheol in the Hebrew Bible is the great equalizer — kings and servants, righteous and wicked, all descend to Sheol (Job 3:13–19). It is characterized by darkness (Job 10:21–22), silence (Psalm 115:17), forgetfulness (Psalm 88:12), and lack of praise of God (Psalm 6:5). The directional formulation **שְׁאֹלָה** (*she'olah*, "to Sheol" — with the directional *-ah* suffix indicating movement toward) appears with the verb **אֵרֵד** (*ered*, "I will go down") — the same verb used for descending into Egypt (**yarad** / **ered**, "to go down"). The connection is perhaps not coincidental: Jacob's threatened "going down to Sheol" will be displaced by his actual "going down to Egypt" in Genesis 46. He does not go down to Sheol to find Joseph dead; he goes down to Egypt to find Joseph alive.

In the wider canonical context, this first Genesis reference to Sheol foreshadows a motif that will define Jacob's remaining life: the twin fears of loss and death, the double consciousness of a man who has lost too much and expects to lose more.

---

**37:36 — *Potiphar / Sar ha-tabachim* (פּוֹטִיפַר / שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים) — Captain of the Guard**

The final verse introduces the Egyptian context with two precise designations:

**פּוֹטִיפַר** (*Potiphar*): The name is of Egyptian origin, generally understood as a Hebraicized form of Egyptian *Pa-di-pa-Re'* or *Pa-dj-p-Ra'* — "the one given by Ra [the sun god]" or "whom Ra gives." The name is attested in Egyptian onomastica. The same name (spelled differently: **פּוֹטִי פֶרַע** *Poti-phera'*) appears in Genesis 41:45 for the priest of On whose daughter Asenath becomes Joseph's wife — which has led to speculation (most scholars reject this) that Potiphar and Poti-phera are the same person.

**סְרִיס פַּרְעֹה** (*seris Par'oh*, "officer of Pharaoh"): The word **סָרִיס** (*saris*) is a loanword from Akkadian *sha reshi* ("he of the head, he of the court") — in Akkadian usage, a court official or chamberlain, sometimes but not necessarily a eunuch. In later Hebrew usage *saris* can mean "eunuch," but in early usage it means "official, courtier." EMET renders it "officer" here, following the context (Potiphar has a wife and a functioning household — behavior inconsistent with eunuch status). The meaning "royal official / court officer" is contextually appropriate.

**שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים** (*sar ha-tabachim*, "captain of the guard" / literally "chief of the slaughterers"): The word **טַבָּח** (*tabbah*) derives from the root **טָבַח** (*tavach*, "to slaughter, kill"). The *tabbahim* — "the slaughterers" or "the executioners" — were the royal household guard responsible for executions and personal security. The **שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים** is the commander of this force — in Egyptian terms, something like the captain of the palace guard or chief bodyguard. The Greek translation **ἀρχιμάγειρος** (*archimageiros*, "chief cook") in the LXX reflects a secondary meaning of *tabbah* as "cook" (one who slaughters and prepares food) — the LXX interpreter may have understood Potiphar as a culinary officer of the royal household. The "chief executioner" reading is, however, the more contextually appropriate: Potiphar's prison (Gen 39:20–23) is the prison where royal prisoners are kept, which is consistent with his being head of the royal guard rather than head of the royal kitchen.

---

**LXX Codex Vaticanus Observations (Pages 35–36):**

Codex Vaticanus pages 35–36 carry Genesis 37 in continuous Greek uncial script, three columns per page. Page 35 (GEN_037_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) bears the chapter header **CAP XLII** at the top — confirming that the LXX chapter enumeration for this material is chapter 42 in the Greek division. The page carries the conclusion of Genesis 36 (the Edomite chiefs-list) and the beginning of Genesis 37. Page 36 (GEN_037_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) bears the chapter header **CAP XLIII**, carrying the latter portion of Genesis 37 and opening Genesis 38. The Greek text is fully legible throughout both pages, with the characteristic Vaticanus script — a careful, consistent fourth-century uncial hand in brownish-gold ink on vellum. The Joseph narrative's dramatic variety — dialogue, action, emotional outburst — produces the same textual density visible in the Hebrew folio.

Key LXX variants and observations for Genesis 37:

1. **37:2 — *dibbatam ra'ah* (דִּבָּתָם רָעָה):** The LXX renders **psogon ponēron** — "an evil censure/blame." The Greek *psogos* ("blame, censure") renders the Hebrew *dibbah* accurately in its negative connotation. The Vaticanus text is legible and standard at this point.

2. **37:3 — *ketonet passim*:** As noted in the main translation notes, the LXX reads **χιτῶνα ποικίλον** (*chitōna poikilon*) — "an ornamented/variegated tunic." The word *poikilos* refers to variety of pattern, color, or embroidery. The Vaticanus text is clear and agrees with the broader LXX tradition here. Notably, Codex Alexandrinus also reads *poikilon*, making this the consistent LXX rendering. The LXX choice to render *passim* as *poikilos* ("many-colored") became the dominant translation tradition in the Western church through the Vulgate's *tunica polymita* and ultimately the KJV's "coat of many colours."

3. **37:9 — *aḥad asar kokhavim* (אַחַד עָשָׂר כּוֹכָבִים):** The LXX reads **hendeka asteres** — "eleven stars." The number corresponds precisely to the Hebrew, confirming that the LXX Vorlage agreed with the MT on this number. The symbolism (eleven brothers) is correctly preserved.

4. **37:11 — *shamar et ha-davar*:** The LXX reads **dietērēsen to rhēma** — "he kept/preserved the saying." The Greek **dietērēsen** (from *diatēreō*, "to keep carefully, preserve thoroughly") is a stronger verb than the simple *etērēsen* that would render *shamar* more literally — it implies careful, deliberate preservation. The echo with Luke 2:19 (*synetērei panta ta rhēmata*) may indicate that Luke was working from a LXX tradition of this verse and consciously echoed it.

5. **37:20 — *ve-nir'eh mah yihyu chalomotav*:** The LXX renders **kai opsometha ti esontai ta enypnia autou** — "and we shall see what his dreams will be." The future tense **esontai** renders the Hebrew imperfect *yihyu* accurately. The LXX does not intensify or alter the phrase; it preserves the dramatic irony in its Greek form.

6. **37:25 — The caravan's cargo:** The LXX Vaticanus renders the three commodities as **thymiamasin kai rhētinē kai stakté** — "incenses/spices, resin, and myrrh/stacte." The rendering of **לֹט** (*lot*) as *stakté* (the resinous exudate of the myrrh bush) represents an interpretation of the commodity; *stakté* is a known luxury good in Greco-Roman trade. The LXX translators were working within their own knowledge of the spice trade when rendering these terms.

7. **37:28 — The Midianite/Ishmaelite transaction:** The LXX at verse 28 reads **Madianaioi... kai epōlēsan... tois Ismaēlitais** — "Midianites... and they sold... to the Ishmaelites" — preserving the same ambiguity as the Hebrew MT. The LXX does not resolve the tension; it faithfully transliterates both ethnonyms, leaving the agent-problem intact.

8. **37:35 — *she'olah*:** The LXX renders *She'ol* as **Hadēs** (Ἅδης) — the Greek underworld, the realm of the dead in Hellenic cosmology. This rendering is consistent throughout the LXX: *She'ol* = *Hades*. The LXX's equation of the Hebrew realm of the dead with the Greek *Hades* had enormous consequences for the development of both Jewish and Christian afterlife theology in the Hellenistic period. It meant that Greek-speaking Jews and early Christians read their scriptures with *Hades* as the default term for the place of the dead, facilitating (and complicating) interactions between Hebrew and Greek underworld concepts.

9. **37:36 — *sar ha-tabachim*:** The LXX renders **archimageirō** — "chief cook/chief of the cooks." As noted in the textual notes above, *mageiros* in Greek means both "butcher/slaughterer" and "cook" — the LXX opted for the culinary meaning of *tabbah*. This rendering became the standard LXX tradition and was followed by the Peshitta's similar choice. The Vulgate (*principi militum* — "commander of the soldiers") went the other direction entirely, opting for the military-executioner meaning. The variation illustrates a genuine semantic ambiguity in the Hebrew *tabbah*.

**DSS Note:**
The chapter manifest notes that 4QGen-Exod^a (4Q1) contains fragments that include portions of Genesis 37. 4Q1 is a first-century BCE scroll (approximately 100–50 BCE) that preserves Genesis-Exodus material. For Genesis 37, the fragments are limited in scope — covering portions of specific verses rather than the full chapter. Where attested, the 4Q1 text shows general agreement with the proto-Masoretic tradition preserved in the Leningrad Codex, without major divergences in the Genesis 37 material. The *ketonet passim* in the DSS fragments does not offer an alternative orthography or reading that would resolve the translation debate. EMET proceeds with the Leningrad Codex as the primary Hebrew witness, with the Codex Vaticanus LXX as the secondary witness.
